title: Antibody screening by IP (Timing - 3 days)
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"13br5","text":"This protocol details the first procedure, antibody screening by IP (timing - 3 days) that includes protein extraction, sample preparation, antibody-beads conjugation, immunoprecipitation and WB assessment of antibody performance by IP.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Protein extraction has two options: Option 1: Cell lysate preparation for IP – intracellular protein ● Timing 1.5 hrs (Day 1) Option 2: Culture media collection for IP – secreted protein ● Timing 1.5 hrs (Day 1)
Grow a 150 mm dish of parental cells to generate enough lysate for two IPs (7 dishes if 14 antibodies are tested).
Place each culture dish On ice and discard medium.
Wash the adherent cells three times with ~ 10 mL of ice-cold PBS 1x. Ensure total removal of PBS between washes with, for example, a vacuum.
After the last wash, add 1.0 mL of ice-cold IP lysis buffer supplemented with 1x protease inhibitor cocktail mix to each 150 mm dish.
Use cell scrapers to gently detach adherent cells from the petri dish.
Collect and pool parental cell lysates into a 5 or 15 ml tube, depending on the number of dishes used.
Rock cell lysates for 00:30:00 at 4 °C .
Centrifuge at ~ 110000 x g, 4 °C, 15 minutes (as with WB).
Gently remove the tubes from the rotor and place them On ice .

Pool the supernatants into the same tube kept On ice . Discard pellets. CRITICAL STEP Freshly prepared lysates must be used for the IP experiment. Freezing the protein sample might affect the epitope to be recognized by the antibodies tested.
Grow a 150 mm dish of parental cells for each IP (15 dishes if 14 antibodies are tested).

As described in the WB procedure 1A option 2 [In protocol ' Antibody screening by WB (Timing - 2 Days) -> Protein extraction -> Culture medium collection for WB '], wash and grow the cells without serum for 18:00:00 , then collect and concentrate media.
Combine all concentrated media from parental cells into the appropriate tube.
Add the corresponding volume of 100x protease inhibitor cocktail mix for a final concentration of 1x. CRITICAL STEP Freshly collected and concentrated media must be used for the IP experiment.
Measure protein concentration using a BCA protein assay kit (lysate) or Bradford reagent (medium).

For lysates, adjust protein concentration to 2 mg/mL with IP lysis buffer. 1 mg ( 500 µL at 2.0 mg/mL ) is used later for each IP. For concentrated media, the concentration is usually at ~ 1 mg/mL . 500 µg ( 500 µL at 1.0 mg/mL ) is used later for each IP.

Save enough lysate for starting material sample. CRITICAL STEP 4% starting material is suggested here. 20 µL of lysate at 2.0 mg/mL ( 40 µg ) or 20 µL of medium at 1.0 mg/mL ( 20 µg ) are kept aside to run side-by-side with each IP. For 14 antibodies, save ~ 300 µL for starting material.
Select the appropriate type of magnetic beads for each antibody (Table 3) 35 .
Resuspend the beads slurry.
Label enough microtubes (one microtube per antibody to be tested) and add 30 µL of magnetic beads slurry to 1.0 ml of IP lysis buffer.

Add 2.0 µg of the corresponding primary antibody. TROUBLESHOOTING When the antibody concentration is not provided by the manufacturer, follow their volume recommendation for IP, if applicable. If not, test a certain volume of the antibody and keep a record of the volume tested.
Maintain constant agitation on a rotating mixer for 01:00:00 at 4 °C .
Place tubes on the DynaMag-2 magnet and allow 00:00:15 for the beads to attach to the magnet.
Vacuum out the buffer to remove unbound antibodies.
Add 1.0 mL of IP lysis buffer and allow release of the beads by taking the tube off the magnet.
Wash the beads by inverting the tube multiple times to resuspend.
Repeat to wash a second time and remove the excess unbound antibodies. CRITICAL STEP Do not let the beads dry out at any step.
Remove buffer from the antibody-bead conjugate using the magnet.
Add 500 µL of lysate at 2.0 mg/mL or 500 µL of medium at 1.0 mg/mL to each tube of antibody-conjugated beads.
Incubate antibody-beads conjugates with the lysate for 01:00:00 at 4 °C with constant agitation on a rotating mixer.
Place each microtube on DynaMag-2 and allow at least 00:00:15 for the beads to converge toward the magnet.
From each tube, collect 20 µL of samples which represent the unbound fraction (proteins that did not bind to the antibody-bead conjugate).
Pipet each unbound fraction in a labelled microtube, set aside On ice .
Vacuum out any remaining samples from each tube on the DynaMag-2.
Wash the magnetic beads 3x in 1 ml IP lysis buffer supplemented with protease inhibitor cocktail mix.
After last wash, elute with 30 µL of the appropriate loading sample buffer diluted to 1x in IP lysis buffer.

Add loading sample buffer to the starting material and unbound fractions. Similarly, prepare molecular weight marker sample (volumes should be completed with IP buffer to match sample volumes). CRITICAL STEP The final concentration of loading sample buffer must be identical in all samples (usually 1x).
Heat all samples for 00:10:00 at 65 °C in a heat block. PAUSE POINT Samples can be left at Room temperature for one day or stored at -20 °C for several weeks.
Select the same polyacrylamide gel as selected for the WB screening (Table 1).

Load samples on 12-well polyacrylamide gels in the order suggested in Fig. S1b. Fig. S1b A total of 3 antibodies can be evaluated from a single 12-well gel. To test 14 antibodies, 5 x 12-well gels are required.

The following WB is done using KO-validated antibodies selected from the WB screening (procedure 1 [Protocol named 'Antibody screening by WB (Timing - 2 Days)' ]). CRITICAL STEP Immunoglobulins from the antibodies used in the IP are eluted with the loading sample buffer containing reducing agents. For rabbit polyclonal antibodies, the heavy chain runs at 50 KDa , and the light chain at 25 KDa . For other types of antibodies, the molecular weight of these chains might vary. The same primary antibody is ideally used in WB to assess the performance of all antibodies tested by IP. TROUBLESHOOTING When the proteins of interest have a molecular weight close to that of immunoglobulin chains, the antibody used in WB may cross-react with these chains, masking the protein signal. These cross-reactions can also create noise elsewhere in the IP pathway (Fig. 9a, case 1 and Fig. 9b, case 4). Two options are proposed to solve this problem: 1) use a secondary detection system that should not react with unstructured immunoglobulins to avoid these cross-reactivities (Fig. 9a, case 2 and Fig. 9b, case 5) or 2) where possible, choose a primary KO-validated antibody raised in a different host than the antibody used in IP, ideally allowing minimal detection of immunoglobulins from different species (Fig. 9a, case 3 and Fig. 9b, case 6).

Perform the WB as detailed in procedure 1E [' Blocking and primary antibody incubation ● Timing 1 hr and O/N (Day 1 and 2)' ], 1F [' Secondary antibody incubation and signal detection ● Timing 3 hrs (Day 2)' ].
Analyse the data by comparing the WB signal in the starting material, unbound fraction and immunoprecipitate (Fig. 5). Fig. 5

Perform the WB as detailed in procedure 1E [' Blocking and primary antibody incubation ● Timing 1 hr and O/N (Day 1 and 2)' ], 1F [' Secondary antibody incubation and signal detection ● Timing 3 hrs (Day 2)' ].
Analyse the data by comparing the WB signal in the starting material, unbound fraction and immunoprecipitate (Fig. 5). Fig. 5
